content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I want to see a movie about hipsters

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I would like to see a movie about people who are leading pointless lives in the urban environment, but who enjoy that. Something along the lines of The Sun Also Rises by Hemingway. L'eclisse by Antonioni comes close to what I am looking for, but I would like even less plot. In other words, I  don't mind if a movie is arty to the point of  being  boring. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Richard Linklater's Slacker and Waking Life are both perfect for this.
2. Frances Ha
  - Probably a lot of films that Greta Gerwig is in or had a part in making/writing, etc. Also films by her husband, Noah Baumbach.
3. High Fidelity (2000) - Cusack, Jack Black and the other guy are arguably hipsters.
4. Only Lovers Left Alive does this with vampires 
5. Swingers
6. The Worst Person in the World
7. have you seen Paterson ? 
basically the perfect movie about finding meaning in a quiet life
8. Metropolitan  
9. Maybe this doesn't fit your description exactly but I'll take any opportunity to suggest one of my favorite movies— Perfect Days.

## 2. Looking for emotional movies that stay with you

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m looking for movie suggestions that leave a strong emotional impact and stay with you even after the credits roll.
I enjoy films that focus on deep characters, meaningful relationships, personal struggles, or quiet but powerful storytelling. Genre isn’t a limitation — drama, romance, slice of life, coming-of-age, or even slow-burn thrillers are all welcome if the emotional core is strong.
Language also isn’t an issue. I’m open to Hollywood, Indian cinema, Korean, Japanese, European, or any other international films. I don’t mind slower pacing as long as the story and characters feel genuine and engaging.
Some movies make you reflect on life, relationships, or yourself, and that’s exactly what I’m looking for right now.
I’d really appreciate recommendations along with a short reason why you think the movie fits this description (without spoilers). Thanks in advance.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. English patient, kite runner, atonement, Lion (omg), Beautiful boy, dead poets society
  - Bro dead poets society is peak.. I will watch the other ones too
    - What’s the vibe like relationships or bigger questions? There are also the classics good will hunting, before sunrise. I have these on my list but haven’t seen them yet: Manchester by the sea, blue valentine and ikiru
2. The Shawshank redemption
3. Past Lives
  - So good!
4. Sling Blade
5. Train Dreams. Not very many trains or dreams, but a beautiful look at life.
6. Brokeback Mountain
7. Breaking The Waves (1996)

## 3. movies with similar dialogue and vibe as pulp fiction & goodfellas

**Score:** 14
**Body:** i'm currently watching pulp fiction high ash and i'm just now noticing all the good dialogue the movie has especially with mia and vincent im just also just now realizing how funny lance is i have also seen once upon a time in hollywood to im a big fan of mostly all of tarantinos work 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A Bronx Tale
  - Now you cant leave
2. True romance
3. The Big Lebowski

Raising Arizona

Adaptation

Full Metal Jacket

Casino

The Departed

Fleabag and Tires are shows but belong here for good dialogue & writing.
  - jack nicholson is scary af in the departed it's crazy how invested in his role he was
4. Sexy Beast
5. Snatch
6. Ocean's 11
7. Lock Stock and Two Smoking Barrels 

Snatch

Rock n Rolla
8. The Gentlemen

American Hustle

## 4. Another but fun Time loop Movie! | ALL YOU NEED IS KILL (2026)

**Score:** 14
**Body:** ALL YOU NEED IS KILL was a good watch, it did inspire the movie with **Tom Cruise** and **Emily Blunt,** **Edge of Tomorrow**, but the movies are vastly different, only taking core concepts, so the good news is if you watched Edge of tommrow this will be a surprise for you.

ALL YOU NEED IS KILL really starts when a huge, mysterious flower called Darol suddenly appears in Japan. Volunteers like Rita are sent in to help with cleanup and rebuilding. However, one day, Darol releases a signal, unleashing swarms of creatures that kill everyone. Rita tries to escape but dies and is stuck in this time loop. She eventually meets Keiji, who is also stuck repeating the same day. And they team up to fight another day!

For my rating, I’m giving it a 7/10. I was thinking about an 8/10, but it hasn’t reached that threshold for me, because the themes were good, but they need to be emphasized more, probably because of the short runtime.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I definitely enjoyed watching Tom Cruise get killed over and over.. him getting squashed by the jeep.. 

What the hell were you thinking?
2. Is this a cartoon?
  - It’s an anime movie that edge of tomorrow was based off
    - I think you mean its based on the same 2004 manga, unless the 2026 anime is also in a time loop?!
      - In 2004 there was a light novel, manga was later.
        - Haha so it was. 2014.  That’ll teach me not to quickly skim wikipedia.

## 5. Movies that show the power of having someone believe in you

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Looking for movies that show the impact of having someone believe in you (could be mentor you, love you, take you under their wing, etc) and the transformative effects of that. Bonus points if it has to do with academia. 

For example:

* Good Will Hunting
* Clara
* Dead Poets Society
* Billy Elliot
* Bend It Like Beckham
* Freedom Writers
* 1 Mile to You
* Whiplash
* The Perks of Being a Wallflower
* Hidden Figures
* Proof
* Ratatouille
* The Blind Side

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. School of Rock!
2. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
3. The Lord of the Rings


>I will take the ring, though I do not know the way. 
4. remember the titans
5. Kung Fu Panda
6. stand and deliver !!!
7. Rudy

Akeelah and the Bee

Antoine Fisher
8. It’s a Wonderful Life
9. Pig
10. # EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which movie is this for you?

**Score:** 708

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eastern Promises
  - I love this movie! Did you like A History of Violence?

Edit... I should have asked if you have seen it?
    - Viggo Mortensen slaps in both.
      - Slaps ass in the first, claps ass in the second.
2. Palm Springs
3. Enemy Mine (1985).

Frankly I'm surprised it hasn't been remade.
4. Secondhand lions
5. Gattaca
6. Contact.  It feels like the movie just floated by the public eye.  Even today it's an incredible movie and doesn't feel dated at all.
  - There was a lot of buzz about the movie when it came out and for a few years after it

## 2. Matt Damon and Ben Affleck Slam Cancel Culture in Joe Rogan Appearance: Worse than Jail

**Score:** 378

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They also spoke about the unique pay structure of their production company for streamers where everyone who worked on the movie top to bottom can receive bonuses if their film is successful. But I guess that’s not an interesting headline.
  - Exactly. This headline being the takeaway from that interview is bananas.
    - Especially if you listened to the whole thing. They weren’t event talking about “cancel culture” as it relates to celebrities but rather Affleck was talking about the guys he met while doing research for The Town and the overall human experience of trying to do what you think is best for you and your family, making a bad decision, getting caught up in bad shit and then digging yourself into a deeper hole every time you try to fix what you just fucked up. Rogan was the only one that actually said “cancel culture” but Ben and Matt were not talking about it with relation to celebrities.
      - So the headline should be “Rogan relates humane treatment of the motivations behind crime to cancel culture, because of fucking course he does.” 
2. Literally nobody in the comments read the article. Not surprising at all. 


They aren't saying cancel culture shouldn't exist, or that is even a bad thing. Literally all they're saying is there needs to be a path back. Access to forgiveness. 


They aren't saying people shouldn't be called out on the shitty things they do. They're just saying taking someone's entire body of work and standing in the way of them creating anything new after they've tried to make amends isn't the way to go about punishing people. 


Jodie Foster said essentially exactly the same thing when she put Mel Gibson in films again. 


And no. Matt wasn't saying prison is better than being canceled. What he was saying was, if you gave people who have been canceled the option to have their entire body of work tainted and maligned, while being cutoff from working meaningfully again, or do 18 months in jail, a lot of them would choose jail. That without a path to forgiveness the better emotional option would be jail. 


It's not a "poor rich people" comment. It's not a "let's give JK Rowling the benefit of doubt" comment. Affleck says nobody should be judged for the rest of their lives based off of the worst of their actions. That holds true for literally everybody. 


If i was actually as bad as I was on my worst day, at my worst moment I would be a pretty terrible person. I wouldn't want anyone to think that was the sum of my whole. So look inwards. Think of the worst thing you've ever done. Do you think that's the measure of you as a man? 
3. We need a Dogma sequel based on today's society.

They could make something magical for all of us.

That movie.. we didn't even have smart phones, let alone social media. Imagine Loki going around having fun based on what he doesn't like reading.
4. Did they *slam* cancel culture? I’m not giving Joe Rogan any views, so I will never know. But this just smells like shitty journalism by a propagandist than anything else.
  - No, they didn't. They basically said that there may exist a desire among some people to want to knock others down a peg, and that abandoning the concepts of forgiveness and redemption is not a good thing.
  - Matt or Ben said (i forgot which) that if the people who were canceled went to jail for 18 months they'd be better off long term

Because they could say "i paid my debt"

They did not say that it was bullshit or no one deserved it lol
  - Matt and Ben *clap back* at cancel culture!

## 3. Happy birthday to Jim Carrey!! He’s now 64 years old. Do you have a favorite character he played?

**Score:** 311
**Body:** I’ll start. Riddler in Batman Forever, Ace Ventura in Ace Ventura: When Nature Calls, Joel Barish in Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, and Bruce Nolan in Bruce Almighty and Horton the Elephant in Horton Hears A Who

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. LLOYD FUCKIN CHRISTMAS
2. FireMarshall Bill
  - Growing up watching In Living Color on TV and seeing the actors find success over the years was amazing.
  - Lemme show ya somethin'!
3. Fletcher from Liar Liar
4. Watched Ace Ventura 2 in the cinema on release and the rhino scene one of the funniest things ive seen, proper couldnt breath laughing so hard to the point it wasnt funny and hurt so much
5. The grinch / The number 23 / 
Cable GUY/ The mask
6. My favorite character he played was Jim Carrey.
7. Andy Kaufman
8. The Cable Guy

Truman show 

Dumb and dumber

The Mask

Ace ventura

## 4. Ben Affleck Slams Streaming Economics and AI Writing, Says Platforms Dumb Down Films and Algorithms “Go to the Mean”

**Score:** 231

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Why are there so many negative comments against a man saying the right thing?? Reddit is weird
  - Yeah I’m genuinely baffled
  - They don’t like the guy saying it. He’s too handsome or something, and only internet uggos are allowed to have critical opinions.
    - Ennit. Fact is they’re on Reddit complaining and he’s out making money they’ll never whilst doing something he loves in an art form people enjoy
  - Because those morons enjoy streaming ai slop and feel insulted they are the lowest common denominator.
  - what i’ve learned is that a lot of people consume a ton of movies, but hate the idea and industry and reverence of movies. 

if you tell them movies should be seen in theaters, they get mad. tell them artists deserve residuals, they get mad. tell them streaming and second screens dumb down the product, they get mad.

it’s like they take it personal

edit: grammar
2. He is 100% correct.
3. At the same time his Netflix movie is released?
  - Hey money is money.
  - He already got paid.😂

## 5. Witness (1985)

**Score:** 187
**Body:** I was quite young when I first saw the film Witness. 

I believe it’s the best performance of Harrison Ford’s long and storied career and likely the closest he’ll ever come to winning an Oscar. 

I’ve watched the film many times through the years and it’s always as beautiful and touching and exciting as the first time. 

As a child my favorite scene was Book fighting the tourist in town.

As an adult I tend to appreciate the scenes between Book and Rachel the most.   

The gun in the flour scene or the dancing scene in the barn with Sam Cooke playing on the car radio.

Beyond the leads, the supporting cast is perfect and many are given memorable lines.  

If you look closely, you’ll even spot a young Viggo Mortensen during the barn building scene. 

Do you love this film as much as I do?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes. I love all of Peter Weir’s films really.
2. One of my all-time favorites. Ford’s best work, and that Sam Cooke scene lives in my mind rent-free
3. You be careful out dere among the English
  - It took but a single line to show Book he was accepted by the Amish as one of their own.
4. The tit shot is missing from the collage.
  - She sure was pretty in this movie.
5. It's 4.30, time for milking!
6. Peter Weir is one of my favorite filmmakers. A beautiful film and also some of John Seale's best work. I wish Harrison Ford had won the Oscar here, it's the only nomination of his career (so far). The only thing that didn't really work for me was the score; thought it would've benefited from something more orchestral.
7.  For fans of Lord of the Rings, if you ever wanted to imagine Aragon as a hobbit, look no further than Viggo Mortensen’s role as a simple armish man in this film.
8. [deleted]

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. "Sans Soleil" by Chris Marker

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Such an upsetting but deeply empathetic movie. I seem to keep stumbling onto films like Persona and Hiroshima Mon Amour where I'm not prepared for the intensity of the imagery, but at the same time it's like having the curtain drawn back on the world we live in and I don't regret it. Footage where people are hunting a giraffe -- it made me realize I'd never really considered what the reality of hunting is. And so there is something to that to me, the way that bridging the gap between the sentimental idea of something versus its reality, especially in the time we live in where so much violence is perpetrated by the state by keeping people focused on shallow entertainment, where war just becomes another news story among dozens, and therefore its brutality is made palatable. 

Anyway this is a remarkable movie but my god what a difficult watch. I was not prepared for it.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love Sans Soleil, the way it conjures imagery and text to reach for something greater than the sum of its parts. The voiceover feels deeply personal, almost sighing when the poetry cuts especially deep. It’s a profoundly human attempt to make sense of an impossibly complex geopolitical world.

The sci-fi tangent mirrors Chris Marker’s own predicament: a need for distance, for an invented perspective, in order to look at history without being crushed by it. Then there’s the list of things that quicken the heart, an admission that after all the theory and travel, what ultimately survives are sensations, fleeting moments, fragile impressions.

It’s probably one of the most beautiful films ever made from found or “stock” footage. A real gem.
2. Highly recommend La Jete by Chris Marker... They took all the stills and made a book out of it, too, which, if you think about it, given that the entire movie is still photographs + Marker's signature voiceover... as a book, it's as good if not better than the film itself... since you can sit with the photographs as long as you want...
  - A book sounds great but I'm not sure about your assessment given that one of the most powerful things about the film is, at the end >!when the images start moving. The structural change in the medium itself has such a unique impact, I'd compare it to the switching of sepia to color in The Wizard of Oz or Stalker!<
3. Wrote a 5000-word piece for the BFI on this, La Jetée and Level Five for the DVD releases back in the day. One of my favorite films.

A brilliant excavation into imagining, or not, a new society from the ruins of the old. Myths and prophecies made manifest in the post-war era.
  - I'd be interested in reading that. Do you have a link? La Jetee is one of my favorite films of all time
4. wait, what do you think is difficult about it? i never experienced that at all and i’ve seen it several times, really love it. i never really felt like it was a painful or difficult watch.

## 2. Eric Rohmer

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I was just watching "My Night at Maud's" and I like it a lot, although not knowing Eric Rohmer's other work I can't tell if he is poking fun at the pretentiousness of the middle classes or if he's really portraying them just like, that's how they are, no judgment. I think there are some very beautiful scenes so far, for example in the candle-lit church at the beginning. I can't say I'm quite sure where the movie is going at this point but I'm enjoying it so far.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my very favourite directors. The thing to remember is that though he takes philosophy, morality and all that quite seriously, his films heavily feature characters who use high-falutin' language as a smokescreen to justify their own capricious and ethically questionable behavior. It's not that the film is poking fun at Jean-Louis' high-minded rhetoric so much as it serves as our window into the ways in which he engages in self-deception, a facade that Maude repeatedly punctures. It's frequently the case in his films, however, that the endless philosophising that the characters do in service is self-deception is genuinely interesting to listen to and contemplate in and of itself: both of things are true at once. A really slippery and fascinating filmmaker.
  - Agreed, the joy of Rohmer films is that you always know more about what the characters want and feel than they do. But yes the breeze they chat is always interesting too.
2. He's great. I like to watch Claire's Knee and just luxuriate in the rural summer setting, the 1970s fashions...

I don't think he's a satirist. He embraces the conventions of bourgeois realism, and his movies are about the bourgeoisie. "First-world problems." But I don't think that's necessarily a bad thing. He's empathetic, interested in humanity, love, character, and fate, and his stories ring very true. He's similar to Ozu (who he admired).

Also, of all the major male French directors, he's probably the least misogynist. There are two problematic age-gap flirtations in Claire's Knee, but they're resolved in a way that I personally find acceptable. In later films, you get the sense that he sees women as people. Unlike certain other supposedly more "radical" directors.

I would say that The Green Ray and Claire's Knee are his masterpieces.

## 3. Considering Torino Film Festival for a minimal, observational feature seeking perspective

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I’m currently developing a low-budget, observational feature film built around duration, distance, and a single exposed body in a natural environment *(sea /rough waves / rock - prolonged exposure)*.

The film avoids dramatic escalation, dialogue, or explanatory structure, and is conceived primarily as a work of observation rather than narrative action.  
**The work is shaped around physical limitation rather than action**, allowing time and environment to carry the experience.

I’m considering **Torino Film Festival** as a possible first festival context and would be interested in hearing perspectives from people familiar with its programming spirit:

– Does this kind of minimal, time-based work resonate with Torino’s selections?  
– Are there aspects curators there tend to respond to more strongly (formal rigor, corporeality, duration, restraint)?

I’m not looking for validation, just informed perspective.

Thanks in advance.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Se7en: An Amazing film who's message falls flat.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Ok, I am ***definitely*** late to the party on this one. I just finished watching Se7en, and I *absolutely* needed to get my thoughts down immediately.

Before I get into my main thoughts, breaking down the movie's message and such, I need to heap an **enormous** amount of praise onto this movie. First, the vibes are *immaculate!* The set design, and colorgrading of this movie are amazingly rank and dark, immersing me completely in the cramped and wet spaces of the city. The nonstop rain pulls it all together, perfectly accentuating the oppressive atmosphere. Also, the music in this movie is superb!

I know the nine inch nails did some of the music, at least the intro song, and it really pulls you into the malicious mindset of the killer and the surrounding city! Also, all the actors are great, but I have to say that Morgan Freeman absolutely *kills it* in this movie (I also love his buttery smooth voice).

okay, after all that, lets discuss this movies themes and messages. Now since this was only my first watching, I am sure I missed a lot of small details that tie in with the themes and ideas of the movie, for example, one that I haven't fully unpacked is that they never truly name John Doe. I'm sure that has some thematic meaning, but it's not what I want to break down here (feel free to give me your interpretation in the comments though!). I also know that the religious ideas and imagery are extremely important to the movie, but I am not religious, and know basically nothing about the details of the christian church, so I can't really comment on how they tie in.

but on to my main point, I have to say, I ***do not*** think the movie pulls off the message it was going for (at least for most part). Now there are two central messages that entwine with the movie and each other, each basically stated outright at the end of the movie, with these two quotes:

"Wanting people to listen, you can't just tap them on the shoulder anymore. You have to hit them with a sledgehammer, and then you'll notice you've got their strict attention". - John Doe

"Ernest Hemingway once wrote, 'The world is a fine place and worth fighting for. ' I agree with the second part." - William Sommerton.

these two quotes represent the main ideas of the movie, that being the increasing apathy of people in the modern day, which is brought up throughout the movie, followed by the idea that the world is terrible, but we got to try anyway, which is mainly the movies conclusion, but also pops up throughout the movie (though not as much).

So let's break down why I think the movie failed its message. and to do that i think we should discuss what I believe to be the scene at the heart of the movie, which is the discussion between mills and sommerton at the bar about sommertons past.

In this scene, Mills eggs Sommerton into talking about his past in the city, and why he turned out the way he did. Somerton then talks about how the city wore him down, with all the apathy to the horrible things around him, which lead him now, where he is attempting to retire. This seems to be a concise summary of the movie's main idea, and if it were just that I would have just left it alone, thinking that I just disagreed with the movie's outlook, however, Somerton's not the only one who gets to say his piece.

Mills pushes back against Summerton's outlook on the world, **specifically stating that he believes it to be false, only a retroactive justification for Sommerton's retirement.** I believe that tries to set up a message that the world is a better place than it might appear, and that people do care, as shown by mills. I may be reading into it, but I felt like that was Mills' whole character. He moved to the city because he wants to make a difference, he cares about and loves his wife, I believe he even ends up caring about sommerton. Even with the murder cases, Mills isn't distant like Somerton is, he may get angry, but that shows he cares about it. **I do not believe that the movie properly wraps this up with its ending.**

Now to speak about the ending. Mills and Sommerton capture John Doe, leading to the crux of the film... the box scene. Now most people will have already seen it, so I won't recap it, but it leads to the final decision of the movie. John Doe represents the idea that people are apathetic, and the world sucks, he believes that to make people care you need to cause harm, and Mills who seemingly represents the idea that the world has some good in it  and that people do care. But instead of affirming the idea that it's been building with its two main characters, the movie has Mills kill john doe, affirming the belief of apathy.

I think that the ending of the movie completely undermined the themes it had been building with mills,  instead ending the movie with a nihilistic message that leaves me feeling disappointed. It may be that I just fundamentally disagree with the message that the movie ended up making, but I also do believe that some aspect of the movie was leading the audience to believe in a hopeful message, which it undercut with the ending.

**TLDR**; mills’ character set up a hopeful theme that opposed sommerton’s and Doe’s outlook on apathy, but the final scene of the movie completely undercut the thematic ideas they had built with Mills.

These thoughts aren't completely comprehensive, and since I'm writing this at midnight, I may not be making fully coherent arguments, but I believe that my message gets across.Sorry for the length and thank you for reading!



### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it was a great thriller, where are you getting the idea that these are the messages it was supposed to convey? Not arguing, you seem so confident about these being the messages of the movie, did the writer/director ever say that this is what they were trying to say with the movie?

I don't think every movie has a message, some are just great stories, that's how I would categorize Se7en, please let me know if I'm wrong
2. The Hemingway quote is absolutely not “the theme of the movie”. It was inserted late in the post-production process by the studio who insisted on it and Fincher hated it. Personally I pretend it’s not there as it doesn’t fit with the overall bleakness at all.
3. This is your opinion, and a pretty basic one at that? Your statements are very, very literal
- almost like you’re regurgitating the screenplay and calling it a theme. Perhaps watch the directors commentary for some insight below the surface. 

I’d argue it’s a nihilistic film about isolation and disconnectedness from family and community, especially living in big cities. Especially poignant now in the digital era where people don’t even interact with each other anymore physically. People see genocides on their phone screens and shrug. 

A modern lack of empathy, empty belief structures - how you find meaning in your life that ultimately has none. As such the ending is perfect (to me).
4. how does the world showing, once again its ugly side, undermines the point? the whole thing is that there are always going to be both forces of good and evil trying to eat each other up.
5. >John Doe represents the idea that people are apathetic, and the world sucks, he believes that to make people care you need to cause harm, and Mills who seemingly represents the idea that the world has some good in it  and that people do care. But instead of affirming the idea that it's been building with its two main characters, the movie has Mills kill john doe, affirming the belief of apathy.'

I think you're equivocating a lot here in order to make your point. Mills killing John Doe does feel like a slight shift away from his earlier optimism, but it's not an endorsement of the idea that "people are apathetic and you need to cause harm to make them care." He's not shooting John in order to "get people's attention," he's doing it purely out of revenge. Exacting bloody revenge on someone who kills your wife is not tantamount to accepting John Doe's worldview. 

You could say that Mills becomes more like Doe in the sense that he's become okay with killing an unarmed person, but that's about as far as his transformation goes. He's not "killing to make a point" as John is, he's just killing to get revenge.
6. Se7en is one of my all time favourites and I've seen it multiple times including at the cinema on release. 

Here are my thoughts about some of the points you've raised. 

John Doe never gets a name because that's the dynamic he's cultivated, he has erased his past through butchering his finger prints and wants to be known for his "magnum opus" rather than whatever petty things that have led him to this point in his twisted life. 

Somer*SET has been broken down by his time as a detective, he's seen too touch and doesn't have the support network to have that offset by some of the good that is still in the world.

Mills character is the antithesis of Somerset, he represents a naivety about city life and the people in it so it makes sense that Doe pinpoints him to "corrupt" rather than Somerset who's already been beaten down and broken by the worst parts of humanity and just wants out. 

I don't see any inconsistencies in the tone of the movie or the points you bring up. Doe wants to prove to everyone (but especially Mills by the end) that society in his huge city has been corrupted, and tapping into Mills thirst for justice but corrupting it into "wrath" by making Mills bypass the legal system is his final message.

---
